1: The chapter opens with a genealogy reaching back five generations — Kish son of Abiel son of Zeror son of Bechorath son of Aphiah — before landing on the key descriptor: gibbôr ḥayil, a man of standing/valor. In narrative, genealogies signal that a new major figure is entering the story. The Benjaminite lineage is theologically loaded: Benjamin was the youngest son of Jacob, the tribe nearly exterminated in the Gibeah atrocity (Judg 20), and yet the tribe that will produce Israel's first king.
2: Saul's introduction is dominated by physical description: bāḥûr wāṭôḇ — choice and good, i.e., a fine young man — and above all his height, for from his shoulders upward he was taller than all the people. The narrative underscores external appearance, which foreshadows the irony to come: YHWH sees differently (16:7). Tall stature was the ANE mark of royal fitness; Saul looks like a king, which is precisely what Israel will point to when affirming him (10:24).
3: The narrative mechanism that will end with a king being anointed begins with ătōnôt ʾăḇad — donkeys have strayed. Kish sends Saul with a servant to find them. The Hebrew ʾāḇad (strayed/perished) is the same root used for lost sheep and lost children; the narrator's choice of this verb begins the providential-search motif. Israel's future king is on a mundane agricultural errand, unaware that YHWH has already told Samuel to expect him.
4: The search route — hill country of Ephraim, land of Shalisha, land of Shaalim, land of Benjamin — covers a wide arc of the central highlands without success. The repeated wəlōʾ māṣāʾû (and they did not find them) drives the narrative tension: the more thoroughly the search fails, the more completely Saul is displaced from his starting point. The geography situates the action in the territory between Gibeah (Saul's home) and Ramah (Samuel's base).
5: Saul shows practical wisdom — 'Let us turn back, lest my father stop worrying about the donkeys and worry about us instead.' The inversion is gently ironic: Saul is worried about causing his father anxiety, while YHWH has already arranged the meeting ahead of them. Saul's practical reasoning, pointing homeward, is what YHWH's providence overrides through the servant's suggestion in v6.
6: The servant knows of a 'man of God' (ʾîš ʾĕlōhîm) in the city — a man of distinction whose every word comes true (ḵol ʾăšer yədabbēr bōʾ yāḇōʾ). This phrase defines the true prophet: words that reliably correspond to reality. The servant's faith, not Saul's initiative, propels the story forward. Samuel has not been named yet — the reader, who already knows Samuel from chs 1-8, recognizes who is being described before the characters do.
7: Saul's concern is pragmatic: 'If we go, what shall we bring the man? The bread in our sacks is gone, and there is no gift to bring to the man of God.' The ANE practice of consulting a prophet came with a gift — a fee for prophetic services. Saul's worry reveals he understands the protocol. The problem of having nothing to offer will be resolved by the servant's shekel (v8), but it introduces the theme of what one brings before a holy man.
8: The servant produces a quarter shekel of silver — enough for a gift. The denominations mentioned here are small; this is not a wealthy consultation. The servant's creative solution moves the narrative: the gift question resolved, the men turn toward the city. Throughout the chapter, the servant functions as the wiser and more spiritually alert companion — he knows about the prophet, has the silver, and suggests the plan.
9: The narrator inserts an editorial gloss: 'Formerly in Israel, anyone who went to inquire of God said, Come, let us go to the rōʾeh (seer); for the one who is today called a nāḇîʾ (prophet) was formerly called a rōʾeh.' This is a rare instance of the narrative's own historical self-awareness — the writer knows the story is ancient and glosses the archaic title for later readers. The two terms rōʾeh and nāḇîʾ reflect different emphases: rōʾeh stresses the visionary-perceptive function; nāḇîʾ stresses the spokesperson function. Both apply to Samuel.
10: They go up toward the city. The spatial movement — 'going up' (ʿālāh) — is standard for approaching a higher town, but also carries the cultic connotation of ascending to YHWH (cf. the Psalms of Ascent). They agree to go to the seer. The narrative pacing here is deliberate: each small decision — the servant's knowledge, the gift, the agreement to go — tightens the net of providence without any character realizing it.
11: At the city's edge they meet young women going out to draw water and ask about the seer. The question is practical, but the women's detailed answer (vv12-13) provides Saul with a precise window: Samuel is here today for the people's sacrifice at the high place, the people won't eat until he blesses it, and he was expected today. The women's knowledge of Samuel's schedule indicates his public visibility and his cultic role presiding over communal sacrificial meals.
12: The women's reply — 'He is ahead of you; hurry now, for he has come to the city today because the people have a sacrifice today at the high place' — establishes the cultic context. Bāmāh (high place) in this period is not yet condemned as idolatrous; the critique of Solomonic-era high places in Kings does not apply here. Samuel legitimately presides over sacrificial worship at a local high place before the Jerusalem temple exists.
13: The women specify the timing: the people will not eat until Samuel arrives because he must bless the sacrifice first. This detail places Samuel in the role of sacrificial officiant, a function that will later belong exclusively to priests. In the pre-monarchic and early monarchic period, the boundaries between prophet, priest, and judge overlap; Samuel holds all three functions simultaneously. The instruction to 'go up at once' gives the scene urgency.
14: As Saul and his servant enter the city, Samuel is coming out toward them on his way up to the high place. The meeting is exactly timed — they arrive at the gate precisely as Samuel exits. The narrative makes no comment; the perfection of the timing is left for the reader to register as the working of divine providence already announced in v15. No one has had to arrange this meeting; YHWH's prior word to Samuel simply proves accurate.
15: YHWH's disclosure to Samuel the day before: 'Tomorrow about this time I will send you a man from the land of Benjamin, and you shall anoint him as ruler (nāgîd) over my people Israel.' The term nāgîd is significant — it is not melek (king) but the term for a military/political leader designated by divine appointment. Nāgîd in Samuel-Kings regularly describes the one YHWH has chosen ahead of or instead of hereditary kingship. YHWH will anoint a nāgîd, which Israel will call a melek — the tension between divine designation and popular institution is already embedded in the terminology.
16: YHWH's rationale: 'He shall save my people from the hand of the Philistines. For I have seen my people's misery (kî rāʾîtî ʾeṯ-ʿammî), because their outcry has reached me.' The language is virtually identical to Exodus 3:7-9 — YHWH seeing the people's affliction, hearing their cry, coming down to deliver. The Saul anointing is cast in exodus-deliverance language: this is YHWH again responding to Israel's suffering at the hands of oppressors by raising up a deliverer.
17: When Samuel sees Saul, YHWH says: 'Here is the man of whom I told you! He it is who shall restrain my people.' Yaʿṣōr bəʿammî — 'restrain/govern my people' — is an unusual phrase, suggesting both military restraint of the enemy and governance of the people. Samuel's recognition is immediate and divine-speech-confirmed: he doesn't need to ask; YHWH narrates in real time as Saul approaches.
18: Saul approaches Samuel at the gate and asks: 'Tell me where the seer's house is.' He does not yet know he is speaking to the very man he seeks. Samuel's identity is still concealed from Saul — the reader, with Samuel's privilege, knows what Saul does not. This narrative irony (the one sought speaks to the one seeking) is gentle comedy of errors, soon resolved.
19: Samuel reveals himself: 'I am the seer.' The disclosure is immediate and followed by three commands: go up ahead of me, eat with me today, I will send you on your way tomorrow. Then the news: 'As for your donkeys that were lost three days ago, do not be anxious about them, for they have been found.' The lost-donkey plot is resolved in a single verse — it was never the real story. Saul's mission is complete before he knew he had one.
20: Samuel's rhetorical question to Saul: 'And for whom is all that is desirable in Israel? Is it not for you and for all your father's house?' The phrase 'all that is desirable in Israel' (ûləmî ḵol-ḥemdaṯ yiśrāʾēl) is deliberately ambiguous — it could refer to the kingship or to some unspecified blessing. Saul does not yet understand. This compression — Samuel knows everything, Saul knows nothing — is the chapter's theological point: YHWH's initiative precedes human comprehension.
21: Saul's response enacts the appropriate humility: 'Am I not a Benjaminite, from the least of the tribes of Israel? And is not my clan the humblest of all the clans of the tribe of Benjamin? Why then have you spoken to me in this way?' The echo of Gideon's self-deprecation (Judg 6:15 — 'my clan is the weakest in Manasseh, and I am the least in my father's house') establishes Saul in the tradition of the unlikely deliverer chosen from the margins. Benjamin's status as 'least' recalls the near-extinction of the tribe in Judges 20-21.
22: Samuel brings Saul and his servant into the hall and seats them at the head of the invited guests — about thirty men. The seating at the head of the feast honors Saul before the assembled community without yet explaining why. The number thirty suggests a significant gathering, possibly clan leaders or representative elders. The meal before the anointing is itself a covenant-sealing gesture: shared table fellowship precedes the formal appointment.
23: Samuel commands the cook to bring the portion he had reserved, saying 'Bring the portion I gave you, of which I said to you, Set it aside.' The pre-reserved portion signals advance preparation — Samuel has been expecting this guest. The language is priestly: set-aside portions in cultic meals are portions designated for the officiating priest (cf. Lev 7:34). Samuel is treating Saul's arrival as the fulfillment of a liturgical anticipation.
24: The cook brings the leg and what is with it and sets it before Saul. Samuel says: 'See, what was kept is set before you. Eat, because until the appointed time it was kept for you when I said, I have invited the people.' The leg (šôq) is the most honored portion of the sacrificial animal, typically given to the priest (cf. Lev 7:32-33). Saul is given the priestly portion without yet being told why — the meal enacts his elevation before the verbal anointing does.
25: After coming down from the high place, Samuel speaks with Saul on the roof (gāg). Rooftop conversations in Hebrew narrative carry intimacy — the roof is liminal, semi-public, a place for private disclosure above the noise of the street. Samuel communicates something on the roof that the narrative does not reproduce. The content is implied: preparation for the anointing, the shape of his calling, what the morning will bring.
26: At dawn Samuel calls to Saul on the roof: 'Get up, so that I may send you on your way.' The sending (šālaḥ) echoes the prophetic commissioning vocabulary — Samuel is acting as YHWH's agent dispatching YHWH's appointed. Saul rises and goes out with Samuel. The dawn hour is the hour of prophetic disclosure elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible (cf. Gen 19:15, 22:3).
27: At the edge of town Samuel tells Saul's servant to pass on ahead while Samuel gives Saul YHWH's word. The deliberate separation — 'stand still for a time, so that I may make known to you the word of God' — creates the private space for the anointing of chapter 10. Samuel orchestrates the encounter with precision, ensuring the anointing happens between them alone. The final verse of ch9 is the threshold moment; ch10 opens on the other side of it.
